question: A user request fans out to 100 backend shards and must wait for all of them. Each shard has a p99 of 105 ms. Roughly what fraction of user requests will contain at least one call slower than 105 ms?
options: About 1% — the shard p99 is 1%, and fanning out doesn't change what each shard promises.
About 10% — the probabilities add up across a few of the hundred calls.
About 63% — it is 1 − 0.99^100, because the request is fast only if ALL 100 calls are fast.
About 99% — with 100 calls you are essentially guaranteed to hit the tail every time.
explanation: The request is fast only if every call is fast, so P(all fast) = 0.99^100 = 0.366 and P(at least one slow) = 63.4% (measured: 63.7%). The first option is the trap almost everyone falls into: it reads the SLO as a promise about user experience, but a per-call percentile says nothing about a composed request. The second option assumes probabilities add — they don't, they compound. The fourth is right in spirit but arrives too early; you need N ≈ 1000 for 99.996%.

question: Your backends have a p50 of 9.2 ms and a p99 of 105 ms. The measured p50 of the 100-way fan-out request is 129 ms. Why is the composed MEDIAN worse than any single backend's p99?
options: The aggregator adds fixed overhead per shard, and 100 shards × ~1.2 ms of merge cost is where the time goes.
The request's latency is the maximum of 100 samples, and the median of that maximum sits at the 99.31st percentile of one call.
The shards are contending for the same network link, so their latencies are no longer what they were measured at.
Percentiles cannot be compared across services, so the two numbers are simply not measurable on the same scale.
explanation: The median of max(N samples) is where F(x)^N = 0.5, i.e. F(x) = 0.5^(1/100) = 0.9931 — so the typical composed request is set by roughly the p99.3 of a single call. The first option is tempting because merge cost is real, but it would show up as a constant added to every request including the fast ones, and the measured p50 of a 1-way fan-out is 9.1 ms. The third describes a correlation effect, which is a different (and later) problem. The fourth is simply false: both are latencies in milliseconds.

question: You hedge at the measured p95 (22.5 ms) and get a 100-way p99 of 116.5 ms for 5% extra load. Traffic then grows until queueing shifts the whole latency distribution right, and nobody changes the 22.5 ms delay. What happens?
options: Nothing much — the delay was set from a percentile, so it keeps catching the slowest 5% as the distribution moves.
Hedging stops firing, because requests that are slower than before will already have completed by the time the timer checks.
The hedge rate climbs far above 5% (measured: 95.2%), offered load nearly doubles, effective ρ goes past 1, and the p99 gets ~20× worse.
The p99 degrades roughly in proportion to the extra queueing, the same as it would without hedging.
explanation: The delay is a constant; the distribution is not. Once queueing pushes most requests past 22.5 ms, nearly all of them get a second copy — measured at 95.2%, taking offered load to 1.95× and ρ = 0.85 to 1.66, with p99 going 466 → 9401 ms. The first option is the exact misconception that causes this outage: a value derived from a percentile once is still just a number afterwards. The second inverts the mechanism. The fourth misses the feedback loop — hedging doesn't merely suffer the overload, it manufactures it. The fix is a hedge budget capping hedges as a fraction of traffic.

question: Three hops (A → B → C), each with its own 1-second timeout, serving a user whose deadline is 1 second. Measured p50 was 594 ms — identical to the propagated-deadline run. What is actually wrong?
options: Nothing measurable — identical p50s mean the two designs are equivalent in practice.
The timeouts are too long; setting each hop to 333 ms would bound the chain correctly.
The worst case is 3 × 1 s = 3 s, and 10.4% of requests finished after the caller had already gone, burning 38.3 ms/request of work nobody reads.
Hop C should retry when it is slow, so the chain has a chance to finish inside the budget.
explanation: Durations restart at every hop; absolute deadlines don't. The medians match exactly, which is why this survives dashboard reviews — the damage is in the worst case (2664 ms observed vs 1000 ms bounded) and in the wasted work. The second option is the near-miss worth understanding: static sub-budgets do bound the total, but they're wrong whenever the split is uneven — a fast hop A cannot lend its unused budget to hop C, so you reject work that would have finished. Propagating the REMAINING budget adapts automatically. The fourth makes it worse: retrying past the deadline is pure amplification.

question: You ship hedging with a 5% budget. During an incident the hedge rate pins at 5% (budget exhausted) while the hedge WIN rate collapses toward zero. What does this tell you, and what is the right response?
options: The hedge delay is too high; lower it so more requests get a second copy sooner.
The slowness is correlated — the hedge lands on a replica behind the same shared cause — so treat it as a capacity/blast-radius problem and fix replica placement, not the hedge config.
The budget is too small; raise it to 25% so more of the slow requests can be rescued.
The backends are failing rather than slow, so the breaker should be opening instead of hedging.
explanation: A high hedge rate with a collapsing win rate is the signature of correlated slowness: second copies are being issued and are not arriving first, because they hit a replica with the same problem. Measured, hedging improved the composed median 8.86× under independent slowness and 1.02× under correlated slowness for the same 8.9% extra load. Options 1 and 3 both spend more load on a bet that is already losing — they are the instinctive moves and they deepen the overload. Option 4 is plausible but the symptom described is latency, not errors; a breaker helps when calls fail, not when replicas share a stall.

question: Tied requests send both copies immediately, each carrying the other's identity, and cancel the twin when one starts. Measured, they issued second copies for 100% of requests yet produced only 11.2% duplicate work — slightly less than hedging's 12.6%. What makes that possible, and when does it fail?
options: The client cancels the loser as soon as it receives the first response, so the saving works on any backend.

Servers can drop enqueued-but-not-started work, so most second copies are cancelled while still queued; without that cancel path the same policy ran both copies of 100% of requests, wasted 53% of service time, and took p99 from 157 to 5514 ms.
The two copies are deduplicated by an idempotency key at the storage layer, so the second is a cheap no-op.
The scheduler runs the second copy at a lower priority, so it consumes spare capacity only.
explanation: The whole economy of tied requests is that the cancel arrives while the twin is still queued, where dropping it is free — which also means they only pay off where a real queue exists. The measured residue (11.2%) is the race window: both replicas started inside the 1.0 ms the cancel spent on the wire. Option 1 is the common misreading — cancelling on first RESPONSE is too late, since by then the loser has usually done the work; the cancel must fire when a copy STARTS. Options 3 and 4 describe mechanisms that would help but are not what tied requests do, and neither is available on a plain HTTP backend.
